questions.question: What feature do Plausible, Umami, and GoatCounter share that distinguishes them from Google Analytics?
choices: They require a paid subscription with no free option
They are cookie-free and don't set cross-site identifiers
They are written in Ruby and integrate natively with Jekyll
questions.explanation: All three are designed around a cookie-free, no-cross-site-identifier model - that's the core of their privacy posture. They differ on hosting and pricing, but the model is the same. Pricing varies (GoatCounter is free for personal use); none are Ruby-coupled; all track anonymous pageviews.

questions.question: You're running `bundle exec jekyll serve` locally and notice your own laptop is showing up as a visitor in Plausible. What's the cleanest fix?
choices: Filter your own IP address in the Plausible dashboard, repeatedly, as it changes
Move the analytics snippet to a separate JavaScript file you remember not to load locally
Gate the analytics include behind a `site.analytics.enabled` config flag and use a `_config_dev.yml` that sets it to false in development
Run a local DNS rule to block plausible.io while developing
questions.explanation: A config flag plus a dev override is the durable fix. The snippet is included only when `analytics.enabled` is true, and your local Jekyll serve overrides it to false. IP filtering is fragile (dynamic IPs); separate JS files are easy to forget; DNS blocks affect more than just dev.

questions.question: Which set of metrics is most useful to actually watch on a personal engineering blog?
choices: Bounce rate, session duration, conversion funnels, and exit pages
Top pages this month, where visitors came from, and rough total pageviews
Time on page per visitor segment, broken down by geographic region
Click events on every link, scroll depth, and rage clicks
questions.explanation: Top pages, referrers, and rough visit count answer the only questions a blog actually has: 'is anyone reading?' and 'what's resonating?'. The other lists borrow vocabulary from conversion-funnel analytics that doesn't map to a personal blog - bounce rate and session metrics are noise when 'success' is 'someone read a post.'
